title: JPEG: How Photos Get Compressed
concept: JPEG shrinks photos using lossy compression: it groups pixels into small blocks and simplifies fine color detail your eyes barely notice, which is efficient but can leave visible blocky or smudgy artifacts when compressed too aggressively.

exercises.prompt: Recap: what does lossy compression do to a file's data?

options.text: Permanently discards some detail to make the file much smaller
Makes the file larger to add extra detail
Perfectly restores every original byte when reopened

exercises.explain: Right — lossy compression trades some permanent detail loss for a much smaller file.

cards.title: The most-used photo format you've never thought about
cards.body: Almost every photo on your phone is a JPEG. It's why a 12-megapixel photo takes up 3 MB instead of 30 MB. But squeeze it too hard — say, texting the same photo back and forth a dozen times — and you'll start to see weird blocky smudges where sharp detail used to be.

cards.title: What JPEG does
cards.body: JPEG is a lossy image format. It works by splitting a photo into small blocks of pixels, then simplifying the color and detail information inside each block — especially fine color variation the human eye is less sensitive to — so the whole image needs far fewer bits to store.

cards.title: A painter who blends distant details
cards.body: Think of a painter working from far away: instead of painting every individual blade of grass, they blend a patch of green into one smooth brushstroke. Up close it's not exact, but from a normal viewing distance it looks like real grass. JPEG does the same thing to blocks of pixels.

cards.title: Why re-saving a JPEG makes it worse
cards.body: Every time a JPEG is opened, edited, and saved again, it gets compressed again from scratch — and each pass throws away a little more detail. Save the same JPEG ten times in a row and you'll start seeing blotchy colors and blocky squares, even if you never touched the picture's content.

cards.title: "JPEG artifacts mean the file is broken"
cards.body: Those blocky smudges near sharp edges (called compression artifacts) aren't a bug or corruption — they're the visible evidence of JPEG intentionally throwing away detail to save space. Compress lightly and you'll rarely notice; compress heavily or repeatedly and the blocks become obvious.

cards.title: Light vs. Heavy JPEG Compression
cards.body: The same photo, compressed two different amounts:

left.text:
Small size reduction
Detail looks nearly untouched
Few or no visible artifacts

right.text:
Very small file size
Blocky, smudgy patches appear
Fine detail and sharp edges suffer

exercises.prompt: Why do repeatedly re-saving the same photo as a JPEG make its quality get worse over time?

options.text: Each save re-compresses the image and discards a bit more detail than before
JPEG files get physically damaged by being opened too many times
JPEG automatically deletes random pixels after ten saves
Re-saving always converts the file to a lossless format

exercises.explain: Correct — each JPEG save is a fresh round of lossy compression, so quality degrades a little more each time.

exercises.prompt: True or false: JPEG compression works by simplifying fine color detail inside small blocks of the image.
exercises.explain: True. JPEG groups pixels into blocks and simplifies detail within them, especially color variation the eye notices less.

exercises.sentence: The blocky, smudgy patches that appear in a heavily compressed JPEG are called compression ___.
exercises.explain: Artifacts is correct — they're the visible side effects of JPEG discarding detail to shrink the file.

exercises.prompt: Which photo is most likely to show noticeable JPEG artifacts?

options.text: A photo saved at very high compression to make the file as tiny as possible
The same photo saved with very light compression
A photo stored in a lossless format with no compression at all

exercises.explain: Heavy compression discards the most detail, so it's the one most likely to show visible blocky artifacts.

exercises.prompt: A photographer needs to text a vacation photo quickly over a slow connection, but doesn't mind a tiny quality tradeoff. What is JPEG doing under the hood to make that possible?

options.text: It splits the image into blocks and simplifies fine color detail the eye barely notices, shrinking the file at the cost of some permanent detail loss
It stores the photo as mathematical shapes instead of pixels
It perfectly preserves every pixel while somehow still shrinking the file
It deletes random blocks of the photo entirely, leaving visible gaps

exercises.explain: Exactly. JPEG's block-based simplification is precisely why it trades a little fidelity for a much smaller, faster-to-send file. Next: PNG, a format that takes the opposite approach — lossless, with support for transparency.
